hypothesis: A generative model trained to jointly predict both the crystal structure and its associated space group directly from XRD patterns—using symmetry classification as an auxiliary loss during training rather than as a post-hoc constraint—will achieve higher structure recovery rates than sequential approaches (first classify symmetry, then optimize structure), because embedding symmetry as an inductive bias during the generative process will smooth the effective optimization landscape by restricting the model's output distribution to symmetry-consistent manifolds from the outset, thereby avoiding the non-convex local minima along symmetry axes that persist even when symmetry constraints are applied post-hoc during gradient descent refinement.
